sentence: Find a substitute material for PTFE that can be used in industrial seals applications

required: Key material properties & requirements for a PTFE‑substitute seal
Low‑hazard PLC criteria – high MW (>1000 Da)
Compatibility – with metal substrates (cast iron, aluminum)
Tribological performance – low wear against metals
Flexibility at low temperatures – maintain seal integrity

extracted_keywords: Key material properties & requirements for a PTFE‑substitute seal
Low‑hazard PLC criteria – high MW (>1000 Da)
Compatibility – with metal substrates (cast iron, aluminum)
Tribological performance – low wear against metals
Flexibility at low temperatures – maintain seal integrity
extracted_constraints: Hard Constraints for a PTFE‑substitute used in industrial seal applications

Acid & base resistance – Must remain chemically inert (no measurable swelling, degradation or loss of mechanical integrity) when exposed to strong acids (e.g., ≥ 10 % HCl, 10 % H₂SO₄) and strong bases (e.g., ≥ 10 % NaOH, 10 % KOH) at service temperatures up to 200 °C.
Oxidizer resistance – Must not degrade or lose sealing performance when exposed to common oxidizing agents (e.g., H₂O₂, O₃, Cl₂) at temperatures ≤ 200 °C.

Organic solvent compatibility – Must exhibit no measurable swelling, degradation, or dimensional change in the following solvents under typical exposure conditions: alkanes, aromatics, chlorinated hydrocarbons, ketones, esters, alcohols, and polar aprotic solvents (e.g., DMF, DMSO).
Dimensional stability – Must maintain dimensional changes ≤ 10 % when exposed to the chemicals above at temperatures up to 200 °C.
Water‑vapor permeability – Must have a water‑vapour transmission rate ≤ 1 × 10⁻¹⁰ cm²/s (at 25 °C, 0 % RH).
Temperature range – Must support continuous service from –150 °C to 250 °C without loss of sealing performance or mechanical integrity.
Low‑temperature flexibility – Must remain flexible and non‑brittle down to –150 °C (i.e., no cracking or loss of elasticity at that temperature).
Compression set – After 1,000 h exposure at an elevated temperature (≥ 200 °C), the material’s compression set must be < 10 %.
Coefficient of friction – Under sliding contact with typical metal surfaces (e.g., steel, aluminum) the coefficient of friction must not exceed 0.13.
Wear volume – The wear volume under comparable tribological testing conditions must be ≤ 1 × 10⁻⁶ mm³/N·m.
Cyclic fatigue life – Must sustain ≥ 1 000 000 (10⁶) compression‑relaxation cycles at standard loading and temperature without crack initiation or propagation.

PFAS exclusion – The material must contain no per‑ or polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS); any fluorinated content must be limited to non‑PFAS fluoropolymers that meet regulatory definitions of “low‑concern” polymers.
Moisture absorption – Must absorb ≤ 0.01 % by weight when exposed to ambient humidity for 24 h.

Regulatory compliance (if applicable) – If the seal is intended for use in medical or food‑contact applications, the material must be compliant with FDA 21 CFR Part 820 and relevant ISO/ASTM standards for biocompatibility and food safety.
Violation of any one of these constraints would render a candidate material unsuitable as a PTFE substitute for industrial seal applications.
